data.title: NHS: Too Expensive Given The Unsustainable Distribution And Bond Market Troubles
data.description:
The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund (NHS) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a method of achieving a very high level of current income from the assets that they have in their portfolios. The fund primarily achieves this by investing in a portfolio of junk bonds, which are among the highest-yielding bonds that are available in the market. As such, most junk bond funds are able to boast more attractive yields than most of the other things in the market. This particular fund is one of the highest even out of its peer group, and there are few things in the market that can match its 16.66% current yield. However, it is generally a good idea for investors to be somewhat cautious when it comes to any security with such a high yield, as they are rarely able to sustain their distributions over extended periods of time. As such, we want to pay very close attention to this fund’s financial documents and net asset value development if we want to consider it as a possible income investment. This article will, of course, include coverage of these important points of consideration.

About The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund

The website for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund does not provide an investment objective for the fund, which is somewhat unusual. Usually, a fund’s website will state whether the fund’s objective is providing investors with current income, total return, capital appreciation, or some combination of the three. Rather, all that this fund’s website provides is a description of the fund, which is:

NHS is a non-diversified, closed-end fund investing in the high yield debt securities market.

The website also includes a statement that at least 80% of its portfolio will be invested in high-yield debt securities (colloquially known as “junk bonds”). Interestingly, the website also states that the fund can invest the remaining 20% of its portfolio in whatever management deems appropriate. However, in practice, we can expect this fund’s portfolio to be almost entirely invested in various types of debt securities. The fund’s most recent schedule of investments provides the following asset allocation for this fund as of January 31, 2026:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

2.9%

Corporate Bonds

135.5%

Loan Assignments

1.7%

Common Stocks

0.1%

Money Market Fund

2.2%

The only one of these types of securities that is not, strictly speaking, a form of debt securities is the common stocks. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund only has equity shares of a single company in its portfolio, Luxco Co. Ltd.:

As far as I can tell, this is a privately held company, as there is no company trading in the public markets with this name. Through an Internet search, I was able to find a privately-held liquor company named Luxco, and a South Korean solar panel manufacturer with the name “Luxco Co. Ltd.” However, it is difficult to see how either one of these could be classified as a Diversified Telecommunications Services company. As such, it is certainly possible that the fund’s equity position is of an opaque company that is difficult to get information on. This would not be unusual, as funds such as NHS frequently acquire equity stakes in shell companies during bankruptcy proceedings that may not actually be operating businesses. It is very unlikely that this position was one that the fund’s management actually made the decision to purchase. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund likely held debt in a company that went through a financial restructuring process and ended up with an equity position in some corporate entity as a result of the restructuring.

The takeaway here is that the website for the fund claims that 20% of the fund’s assets can be invested in securities other than high-yield bonds and leveraged loans:

However, in practice, investors should expect the fund’s portfolio to consist almost exclusively of debt securities. This fund is not purchasing equities, commodities, or other assets on a regular basis. As such, its market performance will more closely resemble a bond fund than an equity fund.

We can see this if we look at the fund’s historical performance compared to the bond indices. The most comparable bond index fund in the market is likely the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK), which tracks the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index. This chart shows the trailing ten-year price performance of NHS against this index fund:

Immediately, we see that NHS underperformed the index in terms of share price performance. This is not particularly surprising due to the fact that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund employs leverage as part of its investment strategy. As Investopedia explains:

Financial leverage is the practice of borrowing money, investing the funds, and planning for future returns to be greater than debt servicing costs. It’s an investment strategy that uses various financial instruments or borrowed capital to increase the potential return on an investment.

An easy way to think about leverage is by thinking about the way that a home buyer uses a mortgage. When purchasing a house, the buyer will typically put a certain amount of money as a down payment (usually around 20% of the price of the house) and borrow the rest. Any increase in the home price entirely accrues to the homeowner, and the homeowner only has to pay the mortgage. For example, let us assume that a hypothetical buyer puts down $20,000 on a $100,000 house. The value of that house increases by 3% to $103,000. The homeowner now has $23,000 in equity. That is a 15% return on the home buyer’s $20,000 down payment. The homeowner thus made 15% even though the house only went up by 3%. It works the same way for this fund. We can basically equate the fund’s net assets to the down payment. The fund, therefore, makes larger gains when the price of its assets goes up. We see this in the chart above during periods like 2020 and 2021 when this fund’s share price increased to a much greater extent than the index did. Unfortunately, leverage also works in reverse, as it increases losses. Thus, once bonds started to struggle in late 2021, and especially during 2022, the fund’s share price fell much more than the index.

In isolation, neither the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund nor the junk bond index look like very good investments. After all, both of them declined over the past ten years, and very few investors want to own an asset that does not make them any money over an extended period. However, we should keep in mind that both the fund and the index paid out distributions over the ten-year period. In fact, the primary way that bonds deliver an investment return is through the coupons that they pay to their investors. If we take that into account, we get this chart:

This chart shows the total returns of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund alongside those of the American junk bond index over the past decade. As we can see, investors were actually better off with NHS than they were with shares of the index fund, despite the fact that the closed-end fund delivered a substantially greater share price decline. This is because its substantially higher yield relative to the index was able to offset the greater share price decline and still give investors a greater overall return. While this is nice, we should note that it would have been necessary to use some of the distributions that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund paid out over the period to purchase additional shares of the fund in order to avoid a decline in the value of your position in the fund. Thus, not all of this fund’s 16.66% headline yield actually represents spendable income. Every investor should, at the very least, ensure that their assets do not run out during their lifetime, and that means making sure that the nominal value of a given position either remains stable or increases over time. Thus, the distributions paid out by this fund need to be split between reinvestment and spending for most retirees.

The website for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund states:

The Fund will attempt to maintain an average (dollar-weighted) portfolio quality of B/B to Ba/BB, with less than 20% of the Fund’s portfolio composed of securities rated Caa/CCC or lower.

This strongly suggests that the fund will focus its efforts on investing in those junk bonds that are rated in the top two categories available for such bonds. This may be appealing to those investors who are rather risk-averse and are worried about the possibility of losing money to defaults. After all, the default rates of B to BB-rated junk bonds are far lower than those of CCC and below. Every year, S&P Global publishes data showing the ten-year cumulative default rates of bonds with a given credit rating. Here are the most recent figures:

Bond Rating

Ten-Year Cumulative Default Rate

AAA

0.81%

AA

0.96%

A

1.57%

BBB

3.80%

BB

12.90%

B

24.20%

CCC

55.70%

As stated, we see a substantial increase in the ten-year cumulative default rate when moving from B to CCC-rated bonds. As such, the fact that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund claims to keep the bulk of its assets in BB or B-rated bonds could be quite appealing to investors worried about defaults. The fund’s most recent fact sheet states that it is, in fact, investing primarily in the supposedly safer BB and B-rated tranches of the junk bond universe:

As of December 31, 2025, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund had 83.2% of its total assets invested in BB and B-rated bonds. In contrast, the fund’s total allocation to CCC or lower bonds was 14.5%. It is, therefore, invested in accordance with its own statement that no more than 20% of its assets can be invested in CCC or lower-rated securities.

We should note, however, that the fund does have to give up a significant amount of income by focusing on the higher ratings in the junk bond universe. This is because, in addition to their higher default rates, CCC-rated bonds have substantially higher yields than BB or B-rated bonds. The Federal Reserve provides the following figures as of April 16, 2026:

Bond Rating

Yield as of April 16, 2026

BB

5.68%

B

6.96%

CCC

13.06%

As of the time of writing, the yield of CCC-rated bonds is more than double the yield of BB-rated bonds. Thus, the fund could earn substantially more income by increasing its exposure to lower-rated securities. However, that would require avoiding very careful security selection so as to avoid defaults. A single default could erase the extra income provided by the other bonds in the portfolio, and it can be quite difficult to pick securities so carefully. The fund’s strategy appears to be balancing risk with income rather than simply trying to maximize income at all costs.

Looming Risks In The Bond Market

Up until the past week, 2026 had been a disappointing year for bonds in general. Over the period from December 31, 2025, through April 10, 2026, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG), which tracks the price and yield of investment-grade bonds issued by American entities, declined 0.56%. Junk bonds declined by 1.00% over the same period:

If, however, we include the past week in the performance charts, we get a very slight gain for investment-grade bonds and a significant improvement for junk bonds:

Clearly, we can see that both types of bonds delivered a very strong performance over the past week. However, this does not mean that bond investors should start celebrating, and it certainly does not mean that bonds have entered into a new bull market. Indeed, there could still be troubles ahead for the bond market. This is due to the very strong possibility that U.S. Treasuries may remain under pressure going forward.

Earlier this week, the International Monetary Fund issued some stark warnings about the U.S. Treasury market. This story was admittedly somewhat overlooked by investors who were more focused on the headlines coming out of the Middle East and predictions over whether or not the parties fighting in Iran would be able to make peace or not, but the concerns of the International Monetary Fund should still be very closely watched and not ignored by anyone investing in bonds as an asset class. This is because U.S. Treasuries are widely believed to be the only U.S. dollar-denominated debt securities that have no possibility of default. As such, at least in theory, they typically have the lowest yields of any bonds available in the market and the yields of every other type of bond are basically set by adding a premium onto whatever the same-length U.S. Treasury security is yielding to account for the possibility of default. Thus, while the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund does not invest in U.S. Treasury securities, we still should pay attention to things that could impact the U.S. Treasury market in order to make predictions about how the fund itself will perform.

One of the observations made by the International Monetary Fund is that the yield difference between U.S. Treasury securities and AAA-rated corporate bonds has been narrowing. From The Edge Singapore:

The IMF pointed to a narrowing gap between the yields of AAA rated corporate bonds and Treasuries as a sign of reduced appeal for U.S. government securities.

…

A narrowing spread implies that the premium investors pay for the safety and liquidity of Treasuries (relative to high-grade corporate debt) is compressing.

At the start of 2019, AAA-rated corporate bonds were trading with a yield that was 55 basis points higher than a U.S. Treasury security with the same maturity date. Today, the AAA-rated corporate bonds only have a yield that is 35 basis points above same-dated U.S. Treasuries. As the International Monetary Fund points out, one possible reason for this is that the substantial increase in U.S. Treasuries trading in the market has reduced the desirability of holding them. Alternatively, investors may perceive that the default probability of U.S. Treasury securities is no longer zero. This could explain why the yield differential between U.S. Treasuries and AAA-rated corporates is narrowing. While market participants still believe that the default probability of U.S. Treasuries is lower than that of the most financially-strong private corporations, they no longer think that it is zero. As such, they do not believe that U.S. Treasuries are as safe relative to the corporate bonds as they used to be. Regular readers will likely know my views on this matter. In short, I doubt that the United States government will fail to pay the interest or the face value of maturing U.S. Treasury debt, but I fear that the U.S. dollars that the government pays the maturing debt back with will have far less purchasing power than they did at the time that the debt was issued. In other words, I suspect that the U.S. government will attempt to inflate away its debt obligations rather than outright default.

Even mainstream financial publications are beginning to suspect that the risks of investing in U.S. Treasury securities are growing. For example, back in July 2025, Forbes published an article by investment manager Dan Irvine that strongly recommended bond market participants to stick to short-term bonds. From Mr. Irvine’s article:

“At current yields of 4.46%, long-term bonds may not adequately compensate for potential risks.”

Mr. Irvine specifically cited a possibility of rising rates reducing the price of the ten-year note as a major risk. However, rising rates are not a problem for investors who hold the security to maturity. After all, the security will still pay back its face value at maturity. However, he does make a point about rising interest rates being a risk even though he does not explicitly state it. One thing that could cause interest rates to rise at the long end is inflation. Should my prediction that the U.S. government will attempt to inflate away its debt burden prove to be correct, then we should expect interest rates to rise, regardless of the Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy. This could be a reason why the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury has risen ever since the Federal Reserve started cutting short-term interest rates. We can see that here:

This chart shows the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note from September 1, 2024, to today. As we can see, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note is higher today than it was back in September of 2024 when the federal funds rate was 175 basis points higher than it is today. The only explanation for this is one or both of the following:

  1. Investors believe that there is a higher probability that the U.S. government will default than there was back in September 2024.
  2. Investors believe that inflation will continue to be a problem, which will allow the U.S. government to repay its debt with devalued currency. Bond market participants are demanding higher interest rates in an effort to earn a positive return in real terms.

I suspect that the second possibility is more likely, and have reached the same conclusion that Mr. Irvine has. Investors should stick to short-term debt if they want to own bonds at all. By sticking to short-term debt, we can avoid the compounding effect of inflation over a long period of time.

The International Monetary Fund also expressed concern that the United States is projected to continue to run high deficits and continue to issue large numbers of U.S. Treasuries. Over the past three years, the deficit-to-GDP ratio has averaged around 6%, and both the IMF and the Congressional Budget Office believe that deficits will continue to stay at least that high over the next ten years. That is an extraordinarily high level for a nation to run as a matter of policy, and it suggests that the just-discussed situation will get worse going forward.

This means that interest rates will continue to be pressured upward, and that is why investors in the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund need to pay attention. Bond prices move inversely to yields, and if U.S. Treasury yields are being pressured upwards by the deficit spending, then U.S. Treasury prices will be pressured downwards by the same dynamic. As was already mentioned, the yields of corporate bonds tend to trade at a level that gives them a higher yield of U.S. Treasuries in order to account for the default risk possessed by the corporates. Thus, if U.S. Treasury yields are being pressured upwards, then the yields of all U.S. dollar-denominated bonds will be pressured upwards. This will push down the price of corporate bonds, including the ones held in the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund. As such, we can likely expect the fund’s ability to earn capital gains by exploiting interest rate declines and its ability to deliver share price appreciation to be weaker than was the case in the past. In short, this fund could struggle for reasons that are entirely out of its control.

The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund Versus Indices And Peers

As mentioned in the introduction, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund boasts a 16.66% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yield of any American or global bond index fund, which we can see in the following chart:

Index/ETF

TTM Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.93%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index

6.59%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.17%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

4.45%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.04%

The highest-yielding of the major American and global bond indices is the one tracking junk bonds issued by American companies. This is also the index whose composition most resembles that of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund. However, at 6.59%, we can see that the yield of that index is substantially lower than that of NHS. The yield of a security is indicative of the amount of income that it provides to its investors, so NHS will naturally provide investors with a higher current income than any fund tracking these indices. As we have already seen, too, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund has done a pretty good job at providing a higher total return than the junk bond index over time.

With that said, it is not atypical for closed-end bond funds like NHS to boast substantially higher yields than comparable index funds. After all, closed-end bond funds tend to employ leverage. These funds borrow money and use that borrowed money to purchase bonds. The coupon payments that the fund receives from the purchased securities are used to pay the interest on the debt, and the fund keeps whatever is left over. Thus, as long as the fund is able to borrow money at a lower rate than the coupon yields of the bonds that it is purchasing, the yield that it earns from its net assets will increase. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund is not the only closed-end bond fund that uses this strategy. Indeed, most of them do. As such, we can expect that other closed-end funds will be more competitive with NHS in terms of yield than the index funds were. That is indeed the case, which we can clearly see by looking at the yields of other closed-end funds that employ a similar strategy:

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

The median yield of this group of fifteen funds is 10.54%, so we can see that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund has a yield that is substantially above the median for closed-end junk bond funds. In fact, we can see that NHS has the highest yield out of the peer funds. While this might be attractive, especially for an investor who wants to earn a very high level of income from their assets, it could also be a reason for concern. NHS has a yield that is 612 basis points above the peer median, which could be a sign that the market doubts this fund’s ability to maintain its distribution at the current level. The fund’s recent performance history would appear to support this conclusion. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value over the past two years:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value has been steadily declining for most of the trailing two-year period. It is currently down 10.22% from its starting point. This means that the fund has not been able to earn sufficient investment profits from its portfolio, and as such, it has been paying out more than it earns. While a closed-end fund can technically pay out whatever it wants as long as it has the cash to do so, it is not sustainable to pay out more than it earns. At some point, the fund will have to cut its distribution to a more sustainable level, or it will run out of money to distribute. In addition to this, the longer it waits before cutting, the larger the eventual distribution cut will have to be. After all, it is only able to earn a certain percentage of its net assets every year. The lower its net assets, the lower this percentage will be in nominal dollars. The longer it waits until it cuts, the lower its net assets will be on a per-share basis.

Financial Analysis Of The Fund’s Distribution

The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0905 per share ($1.086 per share annually), which gives it a 16.66% yield at the current share price. The fund has maintained this same distribution since the middle of 2019, but this does not mean that it is sustainable. Indeed, the steadily declining net asset value strongly suggests that it is not sustainable. However, let us have a look at the fund’s finances anyway so that we can get an idea of how its finances actually stack up.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025. As such, this is the document that we will look at to review the fund’s financial condition.

For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund reported a net investment income of $15,389,418. That was not sufficient to cover the $33,226,736 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

The fund failed to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, NHS reported net realized gains of $3,432,940 along with net unrealized gains of $1,393,734. Overall, the fund’s portfolio delivered investment returns of $20,216,092:

That was not sufficient to cover the distribution that the fund paid out. As expected, the fund failed to cover its distribution over the full-year period.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund was October 27, 2025. As such, almost six months have passed since we last discussed this fund. Most of the important developments that occurred during the period have already been discussed, although the Federal Reserve did reduce the federal funds target range by 25 basis points in December. As we have already discussed, though, the actions of a nation’s central bank are not always the most important consideration with bonds. There are other things besides monetary policy that can have a stronger impact on the performance of bonds (such as expectations surrounding inflation).

The fund’s performance since the last time that we discussed it has been rather disappointing. The share price is down 12.60%, which is a far worse performance than either American investment-grade or American junk bonds delivered over the same period:

This is not exactly surprising when we consider that the fund has been struggling to cover its distribution. We would ordinarily expect the fund’s share price to decline more than the indices due to the leverage, but the fund’s declining net asset value may have played a bigger role here. As shown, the fund’s net asset value is down 6.02% since we last looked at this fund:

The share price of a closed-end fund does not track net asset value as closely as is the case with an exchange-traded fund. However, the share price of most closed-end funds does typically exhibit a correlation with the fund’s net asset value. Thus, the steep decline in the fund’s net asset value and its reluctance to cut the distribution are likely the primary reasons for the fund’s share price decline over the period.

If we include the distributions that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund paid out over the period, it still underperformed the indices by a substantial margin. We can see that here:

Please note that this chart assumes that a hypothetical investor in each of the funds opted to reinvest all of the distributions rather than taking them as income. Even with this assumption, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund failed to keep up with the indices. This can, unfortunately, be a problem with funds that consistently pay out distributions in excess of their investment profits. In short, the share price decline from the net asset value destruction can offset the extra yield that such funds obtain from distributing more than they earn. While NHS has managed to outperform the indices over the past decade, there is no guarantee that this will continue to be the case unless the fund cuts its distribution to a more sustainable level.

Valuation

Shares of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund are currently trading at a 0.31% premium to net asset value. This is substantially higher than the 7.03% discount that shares of this fund have had on average over the past month.

Over the past five years, shares of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund have traded at prices from a 12.33% discount to a 7.84% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period has been a 0.8% discount on net asset value. The five-year average price is a bit better than what the fund has today, especially since the average price is a discount. At the current price, the fund’s shares are actually more expensive than the underlying assets are worth.

Here is how the current price of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund compares to its peers:

As we can see, nearly all of the peer funds are trading at slight premiums on net asset value. The median price out of the group is a 0.35% premium, which is slightly higher than NHS’s current price. However, that does not mean that the fund is reasonably priced right now. Indeed, I would not pay a premium for any junk bond fund, and certainly not for a junk bond fund that is not even covering its distribution. The fact that all of these funds are currently trading at prices very close to their net asset values is a testament to just how strong and widespread the market rally has been over the past two days. Personally, I would not purchase any of these funds until they trade at a fairly large discount on net asset value. However, investors who have a position in NHS and want to get out of it might want to do so while it is still trading at a premium.

Expenses

The annual report for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund states that the fund has an expense ratio of 3.39%. A significant proportion of the expenses was due to the fact that this fund uses leverage and needs to pay interest to its creditors as well as make payments to its preferred stockholders, who are also a source of leverage for the fund. If we exclude these expenses, the fund’s expense ratio was 1.17% for the most recent fiscal year.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund is a very high-yielding closed-end junk bond fund. Its headline 16.66% yield is very attractive, and it is the highest in the fund’s peer group. Unfortunately, it does not appear that the fund can afford to maintain its distribution at the current level, and it will likely have to cut it unless the bond market strengthens substantially. I doubt that the bond market will strengthen to such an extent, given that U.S. Treasury securities are likely to remain under pressure from the high fiscal deficits, and these securities form the bedrock asset of the U.S. dollar-denominated bond market.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Doximity: Priced Like SaaS, Built Like Advertising
data.description:
According to the latest data, over 85% of US doctors are already using Doximity, Inc. (DOCS). The platform has over 3+ million registered HCP members. Its free AI tools are used by around 300k users. That sounds like a lot of scale and engagement.

However, the financials are telling a different story. Revenue growth has slowed to around 10% in the most recent quarter, operating income has declined, while R&D spending has surged. This presents a disconnect that is not ideal. Engagement from their AI tools has clearly increased, but Doximity gives no indication that this has translated into incremental revenue. Given their physician penetration is already near saturation, the company needs to generate direct revenue from the same user base — generally a more difficult problem than improving penetration.

The risk, ultimately, is that money spent on AI does not bring in value for each new dollar invested, instead becoming a drag on margin.

Doctors Are Not Its Customers

Doximity is described as a healthcare SaaS platform, but its revenue model appears different. It is not derived from physicians or from providing some software or tool. The tool they offer is merely an instrument to build a network. Revenue comes from pharmaceutical companies and health systems that pay to access the platform’s network. In this way, DOCS is more like an ad company than a pure SaaS. Their revenue generation capability is significant — for the nine months ended December 31, 2025, the company generated $499.5M in revenue, growing ~15.6% year-over-year. However, none of this revenue comes from directly monetizing its physician network through some sort of subscription. Instead, they represent the supply side of the platform — the audience being sold to advertisers.

That makes their economic model indirect. What they offer to pharma advertisers are marketing solutions, including hiring tools and certain workflow tools. These form the primary revenue driver. Traditional SaaS businesses scale their revenues with paid seats. If that were true for Doximity, their revenue would scale with the number of doctors or pharma providers on their platform. In reality, though, that is not the case — their revenue only scales when pharma marketing budgets increase, or advertiser ROI is hiked.

This becomes clear when you compare trends in profitability. It shows you that what they are spending on is not directly improving margins. So while revenue has grown, operating income has declined from $79.9M to $71.9M year-over-year in the latest quarter, while R&D spending increased 54% (from $22.4M to $34.6M). That R&D spend is going into developing AI engagement; however, since pharma marketing budgets or advertiser ROI doesn’t increase in direct proportion to incremental network growth, their investment in the latter is not yet translating into proportional revenue growth.

That means, Doximity doesn’t have a revenue-per-physician model. It is more like an engagement-per-physician model, which assumes that more engagement will increase the advertising revenue and support pricing power. This doesn’t work that way. It creates a key dependency: for revenue to grow, pharma customers must increase spending. But that depends on whether higher engagement translates into higher advertiser ROI. For pharma companies, the value driver is prescription number growth. There is no metric that can show more platform engagement through workflow or AI tools measurably improves Rx growth. For doctors, that needs out-of-platform, old-school, face-to-face work. An online platform may generate leads, but to close those leads, pharma companies must go out and visit doctors at their clinics.

AI Tools Are Widely Monetized

Doximity could easily add a revenue-per-physician model. By not doing so, they are leaving money on the table while spending most of their R&D on the exact tools they are choosing not to monetize. These are not experimental tools. Tools like AI for clinical documentation, ambient scribing, and workflow automation are already being monetized.

For example, Microsoft-owned Nuance’s DAX platform is priced in the range of $369 to $600+ per provider per month. It is one of the most widely deployed AI scribes. Glass Health is priced at ~$20 to $200 per month. On top of a scribe tool, it offers deeper clinical reasoning and decision support. Mid-tier solutions like Suki are typically priced a bit lower than Nuance. Enterprise tools like Abridge, etc., are priced slightly higher.

There are varieties of pricing because these tools are widely used by different clients for various purposes. The value proposition is clear. They are not marketed as just engagement tools — these are productivity software with measurable economic returns.

So it is surprising that Doximity is offering a similar level of functionality — for free. These tools are HIPAA-compliant, almost enterprise level, yet Doximity chooses to use them purely to drive engagement. Revenue comes from elsewhere.

However, even partial monetization can add to their revenue stream. Let’s say they go for $50 per month per physician for the documentation toolset. This is significantly below even the lowest tier of competing tools. Applied to 300,000 users, that would generate an annualized revenue quantum of $180M. At $100 per month — still a discounted price — we have $360M, a sum that is approaching the scale of their current revenue base.

I understand that Doximity’s existing revenue comes from utilizing this network, so they cannot price it in a way that drives customers away. There could always be a free tier. Alternatively, they could price this in a highly competitive way, which will preserve network effects but still add to their revenue base independent of pharma advertising budgets.

However, the company has chosen not to do that. AI tools are given away as free utilities, and revenue comes from indirect, advertiser usage of the network. This is why their margin is not growing — they are spending R&D expenses on physicians but deriving revenue from pharma providers. They could have done both.

The pharma marketing budget doesn't scale well

Doximity spends on growing its network but expects revenue from those using it, not those who are part of the network. This makes a key assumption that is not supported by research — that pharma promotional budget is elastic and increases with physician engagement. Research clearly tells otherwise - “pharmaceutical promotional elasticities are modest in size.”

Pharma budget can get reallocated, but the pool is not unending. In consumer platforms, increased engagements can directly translate into more impressions and higher spend — because the user is the buyer. Here, the user is not the buyer, but the prescriber — and prescriptions are not emotional purchases. So pharma campaigns have defined budgets, their target audience remains very specific, and in general, budget is broadly inelastic.

This creates a ceiling for platforms such as Doximity. Once a pharma provider has engaged with a physician, additional digital engagement does not typically increase value. So there is no incremental spend for additional engagement. In that sense, engagement — after attaining a certain scale, here ~85% of US physicians — is no longer a scalable monetization layer.

This is why, despite Doximity heavily increasing spend on increasing engagement, revenue hasn’t grown in the same way. Their R&D spend grew 54% YoY, but revenue has only grown 10% YoY in the most recent quarter. While R&D expense may not immediately increase revenue, an annual versus quarterly comparison shows that even over longer periods, R&D expense in AI is not tied in with revenue growth. Deferred revenue has also declined by ~$48M, suggesting that increased engagement is not converting to stronger customer commitments.

Saturation In the US, No Global Plans

With 85% US penetration, Doximity has already saturated its most valuable market. Growth should now come from ex-US, right? Given world physician population over 10x US numbers and the world pharma market replicating the US pharma market in size, it should seem obvious that future growth opportunities lie abroad. However, that is not entirely true.

For one, Doximity has never had meaningful global ambitions. Its model is tightly fitted to the US healthcare/pharma market, including regulatory compliance and other metrics. There are variations on this theme across geographies. Some countries restrict direct-to-physician marketing and data usage. Doximity does not seem to have made an effort to cross these barriers and expand worldwide.

Secondly, there is considerable emerging competition worldwide. China, which has the largest physician population in the world, has major domestic platforms like Ping An Good Doctor, WeDoctor, and Dingxiang Yuan with good networks. An external entrant coming in late will have little foothold in such markets.

The result is a missed opportunity for a company founded in 2010. Doximity remains constrained in the US alone, and will remain so for the foreseeable future.

Valuation Reflects the Wrong Business Model

Doximity doesn’t look expensive on earnings, but it is not cheap on the business model. Current 13-14x earnings and ~9-10x forward EBIT multiples make it look like a SaaS business—which it is not. If you consider revenue, Doximity trades at ~5.3x EV/sales and ~6.5x price-to-sales. This fits the profile of a business that has durable growth, pricing power, and scalable monetization—none of which currently apply to Doximity, as I have argued.

Doximity does not monetize users directly, but depends on pharma marketing budgets. This limits its revenue potential. Growth can no longer come from expansion, but by deeper engagement in an already saturated network. However, that is only product growth, and does not translate to proportional revenue growth.

This makes Doximity behave like an advertising business, not SaaS. These businesses trade at lower multiples because they depend on customer budgets, have weaker operating leverage, and do not scale well beyond a certain point.

If Doximity were to be valued on that basis, a more appropriate range would likely be ~3–4x sales rather than the current ~5–6x. Given its current enterprise value of ~$3.7B, a compression to 4x sales implies a ~25% downside, while 3x sales gives us a ~45% downside. Here I assume no deterioration of revenue, merely a multiple re-rating.

The issue is not profitability—Doximity still remains highly profitable. The core issue is that the market is valuing it as a scalable software platform, while the company itself is not scaling itself as a software platform, but is simply using its software as a tool to build a network for a marketing business.

Conclusion (Bear Case)

Doximity is still a high-quality platform with a very large US physician reach. However, its failure to directly monetize this base limits the investment case. Its core market is largely saturated, and while it is heavily investing in AI-driven engagement, there is no visible revenue growth proportional to the scale of its R&D. Given my brief valuation discussion, I do not see long-term value in the company. As such, I rate it a sell.

data.title: T1 Energy: Bridge Year Masks Long-Term Upside Potential
data.description:
T1 Energy Inc. (TE) has been up and down over the past 6 months. I previously rated the stock a speculative buy, and noted that I would be looking for dips in the stock price to enter a position. I did enter a position on a pullback. Unfortunately that pullback turned into more of a downtrend in the stock price. The stock has been at a low of $2.70 a share and a high of $9.78 during the past 6 months. This volatility comes about due to the strong potential of the company, but also the high risk with it. The company has a lot to execute before it can become the profitable company that bulls are hoping to see.

There is a lot to like about the potential for the stock. Energy demand is surging in the US. It will only continue to grow as well. Data centers and the continued growth of AI will make sure this happens. The company is positioned to meet that growing demand with solar. The company is also one of a few companies that are manufacturing solar at scale in the US. I am a big fan of USA manufacturing companies. I think there is a strong trend for reshoring manufacturing operations. That being said, there are also a large number of concerns and risks. Earnings misses, tax credit eligibility concerns, reliance on related party transactions, financing, and execution on their manufacturing expansion. I can understand why there is volatility in the stock.

I believe the risks are baked into the current stock price. I think at this price point the future potential outweighs the risks. I rate the stock a speculative buy.

Financial and Production Review

The company reported strong growth in Q4 as its G1_Dallas facility ramped to full production. The company produced 1.13 GW during Q4, an increase of 64% from Q3. This brought in revenue of $358.6 million for the quarter, nearly the total of Q1-Q3 combined. For the year as a whole, the company produced 2.79 GW at G1_Dallas. The growth quarter over quarter is really impressive for the company as it was able to ramp the facility to capacity.

While the growth in GW produced and shipped was really great, the profitability of the company was not as strong. The company had expected that the scale increase would improve profitability. Management even guided for a positive EBITDA of $25 to $50 million for the year based upon the strong quarter. Instead, Q4 saw a negative EBITDA of $50.5 million, bringing the year-end total to a negative $65 million. Not even in the ballpark of guidance from Q3. The company noted a handful of reasons for the miss and how they were more of one-off issues. While these were outlined by management, the miss was still really significant, especially for the guidance to be provided only 1 quarter prior. These issues are supposed to be cleared up once the Austin facility is up and running, but either way, the company needs to be able to run at a positive EBITDA with its G1_Dallas facility running at capacity. It makes me more nervous about the guidance once the Austin facility is up and running with estimates as far off as they were.

G2_Austin

The completion of Phase 1 of the solar cell fab facility, G2_Austin, is still the biggest catalyst for the company. This will allow the company to unlock much higher profits by manufacturing its own solar cells. Phase 1 will have a capacity of 2.1 GW of cells. The company currently has to source non-FEOC solar cells; decreasing options available usually means not getting the best price. It also imports these, so there are tariff costs. The company has also had a hard time sourcing non-FEOC cells, which have limited production capabilities. This is where the Austin facility changes things for the company. They can eliminate all those issues. It can also ensure the company remains eligible for the 45X credit. The facility remains on track to be in production in Q4 2026. This is good news to hear from management, as delays and issues with the facility will delay profits and cause more cash usage.

Management discussed the capital needs to complete the G2_Austin facility. Management noted during the call that the project still needed $350 million in order to complete the project. On April 14, the company ended up raising $160 million in capital through a public offering of convertible senior notes due 2031 to help fund the buildout. The notes bear 4% interest and a conversion price of $6.80 a share, a 40% premium to the market price at the time of the offering. This is further dilution for shareholders. It was hoped that further dilution could be avoided through reasonable debt options. The company still intends to utilize debt on the buildout. This capital raise puts a dent in the $350 million number, but it will still need to raise $190 million to complete the project. That is no small amount of debt to raise. It causes a little bit of heartburn as an investor to see the financing still not in place for the project. If reasonable financing does not become available, the company will have to look at tapping equity markets again.

The company is also already seeing demand emerge from the new facility. There are customers who are ready for a USA-made product that they can be confident meets the FEOC requirements. The company announced a three-year contract with Treaty Oak for a minimum of 900W of solar modules built with domestic solar cells from the G2_Austin facility. Management also noted another large customer, who they are not allowed to disclose, who is also lined up for the new domestic solar cells. I do not think there will be a demand issue once the company has the G2_Austin facility up and running.

Outlook

The company is targeting 3.1-4.2 GW of production and sales during 2026. The company will have to source cells during the year. They have had to find certified non-FEOC suppliers. This is the bottleneck for production. Management noted during the call that as they continue to expand their network of suppliers, they are feeling more confident they will be able to source enough cells to reach the higher end of the guidance. The company demonstrated the production capabilities of G1_Dallas during Q4. The facility is fully operational and more than capable of meeting the estimate.

The company already has 3 GW under contract for 2026. That really solidifies the 2026 estimate. It is also more than the company sold during the year for 2025. To have that kind of backlog provides a lot of certainty for the company. There are still some variables for the year as the company operates in the so-called bridge year, before it brings on its cell plant. Overall, though, the strong backlog to start the year is great to see.

The company is set to report earnings on or around May 14th. Management noted during the conference call that Q1 would come in a bit weak as the orders are being pushed to Q2 based upon timing with customers. They noted it does not change the yearly outlook; it is just a matter of timing. I do not expect the Q1 report to boost the stock based upon the financial performance, especially given management's note about the weak Q1. That being said, the overall yearly amount was confirmed. I am mostly interested in seeing the royalties paid to Trina (more on that later), updates on the G2_Austin, and any updates on 45X sales. Those are key points to pay attention to from management.

The company also maintained the future financial guidance previously provided. Once Phase 1 is up and running, the company expects adjusted EBITDA of $375-$450 million. That would be expected to occur in 2027. Then, with Phase 2 of the G2_Austin facility up and running, the company expects to see adjusted EBITDA of $650-$700 million.

Concerns or Catalysts

A couple of items could help push the stock higher or cause some pain, depending on the results. One of those was already discussed with the progression of the G2_Austin facility. One of the other big ones is the 45X tax credit. There were concerns about whether or not the company would be able to qualify for the tax credit. It made some changes at the end of last year to make sure it was eligible for the tax credits. I discussed the tax credit and some of these changes more in depth in my last article. The company needs the tax credit to make a profit. Once the Austin facility is up and running, I think it puts a lot of the questions to rest. The company does have this “bridge” year, 2026, that is a bigger concern. Treasury guidance was provided this year around the eligibility of the Section 45X tax credit. The company came out and announced that the guidance provided confirms and reinforces their eligibility. This is somewhat reassuring, but at the same time it does not actually mean anything until the company is able to make a sale of those credits. Not much has changed since the end of last year; it is just them saying that they think they are eligible. At this point in the game, I would tend to believe management. It would be a really big hit if the company were not eligible. It would erode trust in management, and I am not sure they would take that risk.

The company partially resolved another one of the big concerns I previously had with the stock, being the high service fees with its related party, Trina Solar. Based upon the 10-K, last year the company paid $74.5 million to Trina Solar for commissions, royalty fees, and selling costs. These fees are partly a percentage and also partly based upon EBITDA. So they would be expected to be much higher each year going forward. The company is moving away from its service agreement with Trina. It is going to save an estimated $30 million to $100 million at the run rate of 3 to 5 gigawatts. This will help allow the company to be much more profitable. The $75 million paid is nearly 10% of revenues and over 31% of revenue.

One thing that is still a big concern is the amount of related party transactions. The company recorded virtually all of its sales to a related party in Q4, with 97% of sales being to a related party. This is a concern, especially as the company looks to distance itself from that related party (Trina). For the year the numbers were not so heavily skewed, but still high, with 78% of sales to a related party. The company has been winning some contracts with third parties, the Treat Oak contract as an example. This may also start to shift as the company gets its US facilities up and running. As for now, this continues to be a concern. I would like to see the company getting away from a heavy reliance on related party sales.

Valuation

The company is currently trading at a market cap of $1.36 billion. Revenues for the year were $755.3 million. Expectations are for an increase in 2026 with revenues reaching an estimated $904.6 million. Management did not give guidance on the profitability for 2026. I think they are hesitant as there remain some questions around sourcing cells, which include tariffs and other factors, as well as 45X tax credits. That being said, on a PS ratio, the company trades at a multiple of 1.23 and a forward multiple of 1.65 for 2026 estimates. Those are pretty low multiples, but under industry averages. It is also low considering the growth rate of 20% for the year.

Where things get interesting is in 2027. The company is already running at near capacity at its Dallas facility. Sourcing cells is the issue, which will be resolved once the Austin facility is complete. The profitability will be the biggest factor that starts to come into effect with its own cells being used. Analysts estimate revenue of $1.35 billion in 2027, a PS ratio of 1.1. A really low multiple for a company growing revenue at nearly 50%. Company management also gave us an EBITDA estimate of $375 million to $450 million once Phase 1 is up and running. If we go on the low end for 2027 since it will be the first full year operational, the EBITDA multiple would be approximately 4. That is a really low multiple on EBITDA. Also, given that the company has additional room to expand that number with a Phase 2 buildout.

Based upon PS and EBITDA multiple estimates, the company is very cheap. There are reasons for those multiples being low, though. The company has a lot to deliver before it achieves the revenue and EBITDA estimates used in the multiple calculation. The company has to build out its Austin facility. Once it's complete, it will need to ramp up the facility, which can take some time. There is potential for further dilution before the facility is complete as well. The company has to prove it is eligible for the 45X tax credit. It has called 2026 a bridge year, and there is quite a bit to bridge before the company achieves its estimates.

Conclusion

T1 Energy operates in a market that is in demand. Energy usage continues to increase. The company is also manufacturing in the US, and expanding those operations. Both of these areas are positives for me. The company does have some ties to a Chinese company, as it purchased its facility from them, but I think those connections will continue to wane over time. I like the industry, and I like where the company is headed with manufacturing.

The stock has dropped recently, I think mostly due to the large miss on EBITDA estimates from management in Q4. The company ramped up revenue but saw profitability miss largely. 2026 is also what management calls a “bridge” year as it looks to get its solar cell facility open. That really puts the company in a bit of limbo for 2026. This leaves less to drive the stock higher during the year. There are also some concerns about the tax credit eligibility and sales to related parties. Lastly, there are further dilution concerns for the stock.

I think many of these concerns are already priced into the stock. The potential can be seen if the company is able to execute and deliver on estimates for 2027. While it is a ways out, the company trades at low PS and EBITDA multiples. I am not excited about a bridge year and have some questions, but I think the current price warrants a speculative buy. If the company is able to get the Austin facility up and running and proves it's eligible for the 45X tax credits, then I think you will see a rerate on the stock. It is likely to be a lumpy year, but I think there is plenty of upside from here.

data.title: Lundin Mining: The Copper Bull Case Is Strong -- But Here's The Catch
data.description:
Overview

Lundin Mining Corporation (LUNMF)(LNDMY) is a diversified base metals mining company headquartered in Canada. The business model of Lundin is based on organic growth through expansion and optimization of existing mines, strategic acquisitions meant to expand the production life and asset base. Lundin also has joint ventures with players like BHP which allow for synergistic operations, and all this is managed keeping in mind disciplined capital allocation.

In one of my previous articles, I had written on Taseko Mines about how it was likely to benefit from the copper boom. Here we look at how Lundin may benefit from the different supply-demand forces.

Lundin's portfolio includes operating assets in Candelaria, and Caserones, both in Chile, and Chapada in Brazil. It also has the Vicuña project in Argentina, which is in a 50% joint venture with BHP. The Vicuña is said to be among one of the largest copper discoveries in the past 30 years and is expected to rank among the world’s top copper, gold, and silver mines. The Vicuña project is the single most important driver of Lundin’s long-term valuation and growth trajectory.

In recent times, Lundin has sharpened its focus to become a pure-play copper-mining player. This has been visible after Lundin deliberately sold the mines of Neves-Corvo and Zinkgruvan to Boliden and divested Eagle Mine to Talon Metals. This is meant to enhance operational efficiency and align the company with long-term copper demand. According to the earnings report and company MD&A, Lundin aims to be among the top 10 global copper producers.

One of the primary reasons for Lundin being bullish on copper is that it is a key metal for the ongoing energy transition, and it’s a metal used in electrification, digital infrastructure, etc. The multiple demand sources ensure that demand for copper is likely to remain robust, and any pure-play copper miner and producer is likely to benefit from the upside as well. The miner in January had cut guidance for gold and copper production, which highlights the growing importance of copper for the business of Lundin.

Lundin’s copper production is anchored by three core operations. The Candelaria mine in Chile operates a processing plant with a capacity of approximately 75,000–80,000 tonnes per day and produces over 140,000 tonnes of copper annually. The Caserones mine, also in Chile, has an even larger processing capacity of around 105,000 tonnes per day and contributes over 130,000 tonnes of copper annually, making it a key growth driver following Lundin’s increased ownership stake. In Brazil, the Chapada mine operates at approximately 65,000 tonnes per day and produces around 40,000–45,000 tonnes of copper annually.

The impact of the strategic moves has also been largely reflected in the share price trend, as Lundin has generated a solid 89.48% return in the past six months alone, as shown in the price chart below.

Tailwind

Strategic Industries Driving Copper Demand

The global trend towards energy transition is going to prove to be a major source of copper demand. According to a report by EY, in order to meet the global electrification goals in the upcoming 30 years, 115% more copper needs to be mined.

The copper market is tight, with deficits expected to worsen after 2025 due to scarce new production

The graph below highlights the complete picture of the developing supply-demand gap that is expected to develop due to the slower pace of copper discoveries and longer time-to-production. The current hotspot of copper mine exploration is South America, which accounts for 44% of the global capex for exploration. Even in South America, the global hotspot for exploration is Chile, which accounts for 20%. It is clear that Lundin is at the very center of the hotspot. Moreover, a study by Wood Mackenzie found that there is likely to be a 10 million tonne copper deficit by 2028.

Chile is the largest copper-producing country in the world and remains the epicenter of large-scale copper mining due to its resource quality and established infrastructure. Lundin’s positioning in Chile through its Candelaria and Caserones assets places it directly within this high-grade copper belt.

The Candelaria complex is a long-life, large-scale operation and serves as a stable production and cash flow base for Lundin. Meanwhile, the Caserones mine has become increasingly important to the company's strategy, with Lundin increasing its stake by 5%, which reinforces its commitment to copper-production. Apart from this, the Vicuña project presents a unique opportunity for Lundin, as explained in the subsequent sections.

This has been gradually reflected in the prices as well. The chart below shows the 5 year copper futures price movement, which has seen a gain of 45.36% and is expected to move up further.

The reason for copper demand outpacing supply is the rising demand for copper from some of the most strategic industries.

The EV market is one of the fastest-growing markets, and the growth of the EV market will essentially feed into the demand for copper. The reason is highlighted below, as I had previously highlighted in my article on Taseko Mines.

The high copper content per vehicle is likely to drive up the demand for copper as EVs become more popular. Therefore, it is expected that a copper player like Lundin is likely to benefit from the upcoming supply crunch.

Another avenue of demand for copper is energy transition and electricity, as copper is known for its electricity conduction. With global geopolitical uncertainty in the background, countries are gradually making a transition towards alternate sources of energy like wind and solar. However, the amount of copper used differs for each one. The graph below highlights the same.

However, what is worth noting first is that copper is used in all energy sources, and therefore, a rise in electrification will inevitably lead to a rise in demand for copper. A report by S&P Global states that the world’s electricity consumption will rise by 50%, and based on the graph above, this will raise copper demand as well.

Moreover, the world is also moving towards renewable energy usage as well. According to IEA, renewable sources will account for 46% of energy generation. As the graph shows, the copper consumption of renewable energy sources is higher than non-renewable energy sources. Therefore, we can expect the move towards renewable sources, coupled with global electrification, will lead to a rapid rise in copper demand.

As the shortage becomes more acute and copper prices are expected to rise, manufacturers have explored aluminium as an alternative as well. The table below provides a comparison of the two.

The comparison demonstrates that copper still remains a better choice for manufacturers as it outperforms aluminium on multiple parameters. Therefore, as of today, copper producers and miners can be relatively reassured of the demand for copper across numerous industries.

In addition to the traditional demand drivers, another major source of copper demand is from AI-related infrastructure. The rise of data-centers, cloud computing, and high-performance computing systems is driving significant investment in power-intensive infrastructure.

Data centers require copper wiring for power distribution, cooling systems, and connectivity. This creates a second-order effect where AI growth not only drives direct copper demand from infrastructure but also indirectly boosts demand through energy systems required to sustain it.

The discussion till now has revolved largely around how copper is becoming a strategic metal that is going to experience high demand with insufficient supply. Therefore, Lundin is likely to experience the benefits of the same, given the ongoing trends.

Portfolio Optimization with Policy Support and Strategic Partnership

Amid the tailwind supporting the global copper industry, Lundin has proactively taken steps to capitalize on the same by optimizing its production portfolio to focus on copper mining and production. Previously Lundin used to be a diversified metal mining company. However, Lundin deliberately undertook a move to focus on copper mining. A report by Massif Capital highlights that this move was made from the period of 2020 to 2024. Its share price underperformed compared to its peers, as shown in the diagram below.

While the previous assets were divested, Lundin got into a strategic partnership with BHP for a mine in the Vicuña district. Given the scale and experience of BHP, this provides Lundin with technical expertise and operational excellence practices, enhanced credibility, and lower financial risk.

The Vicuña project has also applied for fiscal stability under the Régimen de Incentivo para Grandes Inversiones (RIGI) of the Argentinian government. This framework is meant to attract large-scale investment by offering long-term tax stability and regulatory certainty. This framework also effectively mitigates sovereign risk and provides long-term economic stability for the Lundin-BHP partnership.

So, the strategic realignment of Lundin amid the global copper tailwind represents a strategic gamble that potentially has a handsome payoff, given that for the upcoming few decades copper will remain a strategic metal for numerous industries, and the upcoming shortage is likely to put upward pressure on the copper prices as well.

Financials & Valuation

To understand the financial impact of the strategic moves that Lundin has made, I decided to perform a financial analysis of the sales and profit margins. The graph below summarises the movement over the past 10 years.

As previously discussed, Lundin made a strategic move towards copper mining, and the rise in revenue post FY20 has been a sign of the same. Moreover, operating profit margins have largely been stable post FY17. However, what remains alarming is the sensitivity of the profit margins to commodity prices. The sharp rise from FY16 to FY17 was because of the end of the commodity cycle downturn, where the peak profit margins in FY21 were due to the commodity price supercycle. While the exposure to commodity prices cannot be eliminated, it can be minimized at best. Therefore, this remains a cause of concern, especially with Lundin choosing to focus on copper.

In order to understand what would be a buying range for investors, I performed a valuation of Lundin. Since it is a mining company and the Vicuña project is going to prove important for the future of Lundin, I performed a sum of the parts valuation to understand the intrinsic value. I performed a relative valuation using the EV/EBITDA ratio to understand the value of the existing business because this is the ratio typically used for mining businesses. To value the Vicuña mine, I used the NPV from the preliminary economic assessment and discounted it by 80% to account for the risk and a further 50% to account for the 50-50 structure of the joint venture of the mine. The summary of the valuation exercise is given below.

The mere 2.64% upside may be disappointing for investors given the strong tailwinds in its favour. However, this is possibly the result of the market having already taken the fundamentals into account. Nonetheless, I would recommend staying invested in Lundin if the investor is looking for long-term gains. Moreover, the valuation exercise has been done without accounting for the external tailwinds in the copper industry, and therefore, I believe that the stock has potential for growth. Therefore, investors can consider cashing in if the price were to fall to $25 or lower.

Headwinds

Dependence on Success of Vicuña Project & Commodity Price Volatility

One of the biggest risks currently facing Lundin is the heavy dependence of its valuation on the success of the Vicuña project because it represents a transformational opportunity for both Lundin and BHP. The project is currently at the Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA) stage. However, it involves heavy financial, technical, and operational investment. Stage 1 capital expenditure is $7.1 billion, and stages 2 & 3 involve $11.1 billion. A characteristic of this kind of mining project is that it takes years to reach full production levels. This in itself is a source of risk because this provides a window of opportunity to develop alternatives to copper in the different industries, which is likely to dampen the demand for the community. Therefore, the sheer scale of the project and investment may involve high risk, and failure of the project may imply high sunk costs.

In addition to this, another major source of risk is commodity price volatility. In a presentation on the project, a diagram illustrates the sensitivity analysis of the NPV of the project.

As we can see, because of commodity price volatility, the payback period ranges from 5.4 years to 11 years, assuming a discount rate of 8%.

Given the dependence on commodity prices, what is worth noting is that commodity prices depend on a range of variables like global economic growth, infrastructure investments & real estate cycles, prevailing monetary policy, currency fluctuations, etc. With the dependence of commodity prices on numerous such variables, the impact risk becomes more prominent. This is likely to lead to more earnings volatility, which is likely to dampen investor sentiment for Lundin.

Moreover, note that in the short-term, a major headwind for Lundin is that this year there will be a compressed free cash flow in the short term, as the miner is in a "heavy-spend" phase, with $1 billion in total Capex planned for 2026.

Mining Cost Inflation & Deteriorating Ore Grades

Energy costs are expected to account for 30% of the costs of copper mining. Other cost channels include labour, transportation, etc. This is a major source of concern, as energy cost inflation could be a major source of problems for mining companies amid the global geopolitical uncertainty and the rising energy prices.

The graph below shows that costs have largely stabilized in recent times after prolonged cost inflation. Moreover, 90% of copper supply remains at a stable cost of production. So while there are concerns of cost inflation, the relative stability in copper mining is a positive indication. Moreover, given the price level, production also remains profitable.

Another factor in cost inflation is the cost of sulphur going up due to the rising tensions in the Middle East. And the copper mining process in Chile especially is highly dependent on sulphuric acid.

Moreover, a study by S&P Global shows that margins are expected to improve significantly for gold, silver, and copper vis-a-vis other metals, as the graph below illustrates.

This serves as good news given the development of the Vicuña project if Lundin and BHP are able to minimize the time to production and take advantage of the margin expansion. Moreover, with the strategic realignment of the mining portfolio towards copper and away from other base metals, this comes at a crucial time as the margins of the other metals like Nickel, Lithium, etc either contract or margin expansion takes place at a slower rate.

However, while there are positive indications, the concern of cost inflation remains, especially energy costs accounting for 30% of the all-in sustaining cost. Moreover, note that another problem facing copper miners is the problem of degrading copper ore quality. This is a problem that has been acknowledged across studies and is considered a problem almost irreversible. Therefore, the need for refining and purification becomes more important, which is also likely to add to the problem of rising mining costs.

Upcoming Earnings Release

Lundin is expected to release its earnings on May 6, and there will be certain factors to watch out for.

One of the key factors to look out for would be whether Lundin is able to meet its guidance for production or is forced to revise the guidance further. However, it does not stop there. One of the key risks mentioned for Lundin is the problem of cost inflation. Therefore, in the upcoming earnings release, the cash cost will be a key indicator to watch out for. Similarly, any upward revision in the capital expenditure will be another metric to watch out for, as any upward revision is likely to indicate a further cash flow crunch that may be a turn-off for investors.

The two most important factors to watch out for will be the progress on the Vicuña project and the guidance on the copper prices. As previously explained, the Vicuña project is a high-risk, high-reward gamble for Lundin, and the expectations of the management surrounding the project would prove crucial to forming expectations on the share price movement.

Similarly, management guidance on copper prices would also prove important to form expectations on profitability, as the financial analysis showed; profit margins are dependent on copper price cycles.

Conclusion

Lundin Mining Corporation sits at a comfortable juncture today as it is positioned to benefit from energy transition, growing popularity of EVs, rising global infrastructure, and electrification because all these factors lead to increased demand for copper. Lundin has smartly repositioned to focus on copper mining and production. Moreover, Lundin has been smart enough to get into a partnership with an experienced player—BHP. This has come at a time when there is government support in a region that is considered the hotspot of copper mines—South America.

However, Lundin is not without its risks. It currently is in a position where it is exposed to commodity price risk, and the capital-intensive nature of the Vicuña project adds to the same as well. The project is essentially a high-risk, high-reward project. Having said that, copper prices remain high to support all-in sustaining costs, and the risk of the Vicuña project is distributed through a 50-50 joint venture. So, my recommendation for Lundin Mining would be a BUY.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: IPO Calendar Heats Up As AI Chipmaker Cerebras Rejoins The Public Pipeline
data.description:
The IPO market heated up this past week, with five listings raising a combined $4.5 billion. The group featured two billion-dollar deals, including the year’s largest offering to date, as well as the largest US biotech IPO ever. In the pipeline, Cerebras Systems (CBRS) led the week’s additions, filing for an estimated $2 billion offering.

HVAC giant Madison Air Solutions (MAIR) completed the year’s largest IPO to date, pricing at the high end to raise $2.2 billion at a $13.4 billion market cap. The company develops and manufactures mission-critical indoor air quality and air-management technologies for commercial and residential environments, with growing demand from data centers. Madison Air’s backlog more than doubled to $2 billion in 2025, although much of its recent growth has come from acquisitions that have left it highly levered. Madison Air finished the week up 20%.

Defense electronics manufacturer Arxis (ARXS) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $1.1 billion at an $11.6 billion market cap. The company designs and manufactures specialized electronic and mechanical components with applications in aerospace, defense, and space systems, as well as medical technology, semiconductor testing, and industrial automation. Arxis is profitable with expanding EBITDA margins and free cash flow, although it has some revenue concentration and is exposed to the cyclical nature of aerospace and defense spending. Arxis finished up 36%.

Obesity biotech Kailera Therapeutics (KLRA) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $625 million at a $2.0 billion market cap, in the largest US biotech IPO by proceeds ever. Kailera’s lead candidate, ribupatide, is currently being evaluated in global Phase 3 trials as a once-weekly injectable GLP-1/glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor dual agonist peptide. The company is expanding its ribupatide franchise by developing a once-daily oral tablet formulation. Kailera finished up 63%.

Defense systems developer Aevex (AVEX) priced above the midpoint to raise $320 million at a $2.2 billion market cap. Primarily serving the US government, Aevex develops unmanned and autonomous systems for defense applications, with a focus on AI-enabled aerial platforms used in missions such as surveillance, reconnaissance, and precision strike. While it has historically generated a large portion of sales from two programs, the company should continue to benefit from increasing defense spending. Aevex finished up 35%.

Protein biomarker detection platform Alamar Biosciences (ALMR) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $191 million at a $1.2 billion market cap. The company’s proteomics platform combines proprietary instruments, consumables, and software to detect and analyze protein biomarkers at very low concentrations in biological samples such as blood. With a strong base of 300+ customers, Alamar saw revenue and gross margin explode in 2025, though it remains unprofitable. Alamar finished up 29%.
